stem: The tangent plane to the surface \( z = [[a]]x^{2} + [[b]]y^{2} + ([[c]])xy + ([[d]]) \) at the point where \( (x, y) = ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \) can be written in the form \( z = \alpha x + \beta y + \gamma \). What is the constant term \( \gamma \)?
distractor_rationales: This is the height \( f([[x0]], [[y0]]) \); the constant term also subtracts the linear parts.
This adds the linear corrections instead of subtracting them.
This is the coefficient \( \alpha = f_{x} \), not the constant term \( \gamma \).
This is the coefficient \( \beta = f_{y} \), not the constant term \( \gamma \).
This keeps only the additive constant of \( f \), ignoring the rest of the expansion.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.

stem: The surface \( [[a]]x^{2} + [[b]]y^{2} + [[c]]z^{2} = [[k]] \) is given implicitly by \( F(x,y,z) = [[a]]x^{2}+[[b]]y^{2}+[[c]]z^{2}-[[k]] \). The tangent plane at the point \( ([[x0]], [[y0]], [[z0]]) \), which lies on the surface, is \( F_{x}(x-[[x0]])+F_{y}(y-[[y0]])+F_{z}(z-[[z0]])=0 \), which simplifies to \( [[a]][[x0]]x + [[b]][[y0]]y + [[c]][[z0]]z = [[k]] \). What is the \( z \)-intercept of this tangent plane (its \( z \)-value when \( x=0, y=0 \))?
distractor_rationales: This uses the \( x \)-coefficient \( a x_{0} \) in the denominator instead of the \( z \)-coefficient \( c z_{0} \).
This inverts the correct ratio.
This assumes the tangent plane has the same \( z \)-intercept as the point itself.
This introduces an extra factor of 2 in the denominator.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: The surface \( xyz = [[k]] \) is given implicitly by \( F(x,y,z) = xyz - [[k]] \). The tangent plane at the point \( ([[x0]], [[y0]], [[z0]]) \), which lies on the surface, can be written in the form \( Ax + By + Cz = D \) using \( F_{x}(x-[[x0]])+F_{y}(y-[[y0]])+F_{z}(z-[[z0]])=0 \). What is the coefficient \( C \) (that is, \( F_{z} \) evaluated at that point)?
solution: The partials of \( F=xyz-[[k]] \) are \( F_{x}=yz \), \( F_{y}=xz \), \( F_{z}=xy \). At \( ([[x0]], [[y0]], [[z0]]) \), the coefficient of \( z \) in the tangent plane is \( F_{z} = ([[x0]])([[y0]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( F_{x} = yz \), the coefficient of \( x \), not \( F_{z} \).
This is \( F_{y} = xz \), the coefficient of \( y \), not \( F_{z} \).
This returns the constant \( k \) instead of the coefficient \( F_{z} \).
This adds \( x_{0} \) and \( y_{0} \) instead of multiplying them.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.

stem: The tangent plane to the surface \( z = [[a]]e^{x} + [[b]]y^{2} \) at the point where \( (x, y) = (0, [[y0]]) \) (note \( e^{0}=1 \)) can be written in the form \( z = \alpha x + \beta y + \gamma \). What is the constant term \( \gamma \)?
distractor_rationales: This is the surface height \( f(0,[[y0]]) \) itself, not the tangent plane's constant term.
This flips the sign of the squared term when computing the height.
This drops the \( y \)-dependent part of the height entirely.
This is the coefficient \( \beta = f_{y} \), not the constant term \( \gamma \).
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.

stem: The cylindrical surface \( x^{2} + y^{2} = [[radius]]^{2} \) (independent of \( z \)) is given implicitly by \( F(x,y,z) = x^{2}+y^{2}-[[radius]]^{2} \). The tangent plane at the point \( ([[x0]], [[y0]], 0) \) is \( F_{x}(x-[[x0]]) + F_{y}(y-[[y0]]) = 0 \), which simplifies to \( [[x0]]x + [[y0]]y = [[radius]]^{2} \). What is the distance from the origin to this tangent plane?
solution: The plane \( [[x0]]x+[[y0]]y+0\cdot z = [[radius]]^{2} \) has distance from the origin \( \dfrac{[[radius]]^{2}}{\sqrt{[[x0]]^{2}+[[y0]]^{2}}} \). Since \( ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \) lies on the circle of radius \( [[radius]] \), \( \sqrt{[[x0]]^{2}+[[y0]]^{2}} = [[radius]] \), so the distance is \( [[radius]]^{2}/[[radius]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to divide by the normal vector's magnitude, leaving \( [[radius]]^{2} \).
This doubles the correct distance.
This halves the correct distance.
This takes the square root of the radius instead of recognizing the magnitude cancels exactly.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: A surface is given parametrically by \( \mathbf{r}(u, v) = \langle u,\ v,\ [[a]]u^{2} + [[b]]v^{2} + ([[c]])uv \rangle \). The tangent plane at \( (u,v)=([[u0]],[[v0]]) \) is spanned by \( \mathbf{r}_{u} \) and \( \mathbf{r}_{v} \), and can be written \( z = Ax + By + \gamma \) using \( x=u, y=v \). What is the constant term \( \gamma \) (that is, the plane's \( z \)-intercept)?
solution: Here \( z_{0} = [[a]]([[u0]])^{2}+[[b]]([[v0]])^{2}+([[c]])([[u0]])([[v0]]) \), and the plane's coefficients are \( A=-(2([[a]])([[u0]])+([[c]])([[v0]])) \), \( B=-(2([[b]])([[v0]])+([[c]])([[u0]])) \), giving \( z = z_{0}+A(x-[[u0]])+B(y-[[v0]]) \). Expanding and collecting the constant term (using the fact that \( z_{0} \) is homogeneous of degree 2 in \( u,v \)) gives \( \gamma = -z_{0} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the surface height \( z_{0} \) itself, without the sign flip that the tangent-plane expansion produces.
This doubles the surface height instead of negating it.
This is the coefficient \( A \), not the constant term \( \gamma \).
This is the coefficient \( B \), not the constant term \( \gamma \).
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.

stem: For the surface \( z = [[a]]x^{2} + ([[c]])xy \), the tangent plane at a point \( (x, y) = (x_{0}, [[y0]]) \) has the form \( z = \alpha x + \beta y + \gamma \), where the coefficient \( \alpha \) equals \( f_{x}(x_{0}, [[y0]]) \). For what value of \( x_{0} \) is \( \alpha = 0 \) (that is, the tangent plane is momentarily "horizontal" in the \( x \)-direction)?
distractor_rationales: This drops the negative sign that comes from moving the term to the other side.
This forgets to divide by 2 when solving for \( x_{0} \).
This inverts the correct ratio.
This uses \( y_{0} \) itself, ignoring the actual equation to be solved.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
